This is Major West McFarron. If you were in Texas, as I was recently, and tuned in your
shortwave radio on the right frequency, you might hear some very, very strange melodies
coming from the skies of that lone star state near San Antonio, home of the historic
Alamo.
No, you wouldn't be picking up Paris. Your transmitting station would be much closer than that.
As a matter of fact, probably in one of those big Texas clouds directly overhead, where
some lusty young French air cadet from Randolph Air Force Base, with his radio switch inadvertently
turned on, is happily greeting the morning sun.
What are French cadets doing at Randolph?
They're the first contingent of the some 1,300 foreign military personnel who will be training
in this country by the end of June under the Mutual Defense Assistance Program.
that program designed to bolster the defenses of the North Atlantic Treaty nations.
There are 75 of these cadets, bright-eyed, eager, and fresh from their beloved France for
the first time, almost none of them speaking any but their own tongue.
Now this posed quite a problem for our instructors at Randall, almost none of whom spoke any
but English, all of which led to some very fast French classes for the majors, captains
and lieutenants of the faculty staff.
To their own surprise, by the time their young French charges arrived a month ago, the
instructors were ready.
Now Americans being nomads at heart are pretty much at home after a while almost anywhere
they go, but a Frenchman out of France, and especially a young Frenchman.
In our country, he finds many things strange to him.
Some not so easy to become accustomed to.
For example, their first meal at Randolph was quite a shock to the ruddy cheek young
galeaks.
No wine.

The GI haircut, to which all cadets must submit, also caused considerable concern among
these guests from France, where the closer the hair is crap, well, where they just don't
like short haircuts.
But crew cuts, like all other dissimilarities, were well on the way toward being resolved
by the time the young cadets began their pre-flight training in the classrooms at Randall.
And by this past midweek, Colonel John G. Fowler, commanding officer of the base, was able
to report.
In spite of language difficulties, the integration of the French students into the basic pilot
training program here at Randolph Air Force Base is progressing rapidly and smoothly.
Their willingness to learn from their American instructors is proving greatly instrumental
in accelerating the French training project.

From St. Joseph, Missouri, the Department of Defense has presented the Air Force Hour
featuring the United States Air Force fan Lieutenant Colonel George S. Howard Conductor
and the singing sergeants under the direction of ward office of Robert Landers, music arrangements
by Sergeant Fred Kepner.
The Air Force Hour is produced, written and directed by Captain Mark Thee Miranda, assisted
by ward off the Johnny O'Sullietus, and is heard overseas through the Armed Forces Radio
